Generated using GeminiMember-only story

JavaScript teams are quietly tearing LangChain.js out of production this year. The migration data says quality stays flat and the bill drops. What that actually means is the framework you onboarded in 2023 has become a tax on every request you ship.

8 min read
2 hours ago

--

I spent a week reading about the public migration reports from the half of 2026 and I noticed something that is hard to ignore. Senior engineers at companies that started using LangChain.js in 2023, when it was the way to connect JavaScript to a language model are now writing detailed reports about removing it. They are replacing it with a combination of the Vercel AI SDK direct provider SDKs and 200 lines of native TypeScript code that does what 2,000 lines of abstraction used to do.
The numbers in these reports are consistent enough to be taken. The time it takes to get a response is reduced by an amount. The cost per request is also reduced by an amount. The quality of the response stays about the same. New engineers can start working on it in hours of weeks. The results are very similar, across types of work.
